message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-019: Gate.evaluate accesses decision.approved without confirming the precondition returned a GateDecision
File: src/assay_engine/orchestration/gates.py:88
Description: Gate.evaluate calls self.precondition(context) (gates.py:87) then immediately reads decision.approved (:88) and decision.reason (:89). precondition is a Callable on the adapter trust boundary; a study that returns a bool/None/any non-GateDecision (easy mistake: returning True instead of GateDecision.approved(...)) triggers an opaque AttributeError ('bool' object has no attribute 'approved') rather than the module's typed GateError. Same contract-asymmetry class pass 3 closed for confirmer returns (#F-021) but left open for gate preconditions. The module guarantees 'a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision' with GateError as the documented failure type.
PoC: Gate(name='g', frm=..., to=..., precondition=lambda ctx: True).evaluate({}) -> AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved' (expected GateError).
Suggested fix: After calling: if not isinstance(decision, GateDecision): raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} precondition returned {type(decision).__name__}, expected GateDecision"). Optionally wrap the call to normalize a raising precondition into GateError.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Governance gates — deterministic, code-enforced transition checks.
2	
3	A gate guards a transition between phases. It evaluates code-enforced preconditions (the
4	firewalls, lock state) and, where the governance model requires it, a recorded human
5	approval. A gate cannot be silently bypassed: a transition is legal only if the gate returns
6	an ``approved`` decision, and a ``requires_human`` gate refuses to pass unless a provenance
7	recorder is supplied to capture the decision (audit pass 1, issue #11).
8	
9	Two complementary gate mechanisms exist (ADR-0010):
10	
11	- This ``Gate``/``evaluate`` model is the **declarative precondition check** — mode-aware
12	  transition legality (issue #9) plus a fail-loud human-approval hook that refuses to pass a
13	  ``requires_human`` gate without a :class:`~assay_engine.provenance.ProvenanceTrail`-backed
14	  recorder. It is used by a study that builds its own LangGraph topology with
15	  :func:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gatenode.make_gate_node` for durable interrupt/resume.
16	- The composed runner :func:`assay_engine.pipeline.run_study` uses the *synchronous*
17	  ``GateReview``/``GateHandler`` model for in-process governance (a handler may approve, block,
18	  or be wired to park). Both record every decision to the append-only provenance trail, which
19	  **is** now wired (:mod:`assay_engine.provenance`) — the present-tense guarantees in
20	  GOVERNANCE.md hold. Use the graph mechanism when you need cross-process durable parking; use
21	  the runner handler for a straight-line governed run.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
27	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
28	
29	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
30	from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyMode
31	from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase, legal_transition
32	
33	ALL_MODES: frozenset[StudyMode] = frozenset(StudyMode)
34	ProvenanceRecorder = Callable[["GateDecision"], None]
35	
36	
37	class GateError(RuntimeError):
38	    """Raised when a transition is attempted through a gate that did not approve it."""
39	
40	
41	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
42	class GateDecision:
43	    approved: bool
44	    gate: str
45	    reason: str
46	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
47	
48	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
49	        object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", freeze_mapping(self.evidence))
50	
51	
52	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
53	class Gate:
54	    """A guard on the transition ``frm -> to``.
55	
56	    ``precondition`` is a pure, code-enforced check over the run context returning a
57	    :class:`GateDecision`. ``modes`` scopes which study modes this gate applies to (default:
58	    the full pipeline). ``requires_human`` marks gates that additionally need a recorded
59	    operator approval, which is enforced fail-loud at :meth:`evaluate`.
60	    """
61	
62	    name: str
63	    frm: Phase
64	    to: Phase
65	    precondition: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], GateDecision]
66	    requires_human: bool = False
67	    modes: frozenset[StudyMode] = ALL_MODES
68	
69	    def evaluate(
70	        self,
71	        context: Mapping[str, Any],
72	        *,
73	        study_modes: frozenset[StudyMode] | None = None,
74	        record: ProvenanceRecorder | None = None,
75	    ) -> GateDecision:
76	        # The gate's own `modes` is a self-declaration. When the orchestration layer supplies
77	        # the running study's actual modes, the transition must be legal under THOSE modes —
78	        # so a discovery-only run cannot pass a CONFIRM->ADJUDICATE gate even if the gate
79	        # over-declares its modes (audit pass 2, issue #23). Fall back to self.modes only
80	        # until the runner is wired.
81	        effective_modes = self.modes if study_modes is None else (study_modes & self.modes)
82	        if not legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, effective_modes):
83	            raise GateError(
84	                f"gate {self.name!r} declares an illegal transition for the effective modes: "
85	                f"{self.frm.name} -> {self.to.name}"
86	            )
87	        decision = self.precondition(context)
88	        if not decision.approved:
89	            raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} blocked transition: {decision.reason}")
90	        if self.requires_human and record is None:
91	            # Fail loud: a human-in-the-loop gate must not pass without a recorder to
92	            # capture the approval to the (eventual) append-only provenance trail.
93	            raise GateError(
94	                f"gate {self.name!r} requires recorded human approval but no provenance "
95	                "recorder was supplied"
96	            )
97	        if record is not None:
98	            record(decision)
99	        return decision
100

content.text: The code matches the finding exactly. Line 87 calls `self.precondition(context)`, line 88 immediately reads `decision.approved` with no type check. Let me verify the claimed pass-3 precedent for the confirmer (#F-021) to assess whether this is a genuine contract-asymmetry.

content.text: The pass-3 precedent (#F-021) is confirmed real. Let me view the exact confirmer pattern that was applied, to confirm the asymmetry claim.

558	                for h in hypotheses:
559	                    verdict = plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out)  # only the held-out partition
560	                    # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing
561	                    # verdict.hypothesis_id would raise an opaque AttributeError instead of the
562	                    # documented typed error (pass 3, #F-021). Guard the contract explicitly.

content.text:
Confirmed. The asymmetry is real and concrete:

- **#F-021 (pass 3)** added an explicit `isinstance(verdict, Verdict)` guard at pipeline.py:563 for the confirmer adapter callable, exactly to convert an opaque AttributeError into a documented typed error. There are two such guards (pipeline.py:563, adjudication.py:255).
- **G-019**: `Gate.evaluate` at gates.py:87-89 calls the analogous adapter callable `self.precondition(context)` and immediately dereferences `decision.approved` with no `isinstance(decision, GateDecision)` guard.

`precondition` is a `Callable` supplied by the study (adapter trust boundary), documented to return a `GateDecision`. A study returning `True` (the easy mistake of `lambda ctx: True` instead of `GateDecision.approved(...)`) yields `AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved'` at line 88, not the module's documented `GateError`. The module docstring (lines 5-6) explicitly guarantees the failure type is `GateError` ("a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision"). The PoC is directly reproducible from the code as written.

This is the same contract-asymmetry class the team already deemed worth fixing in pass 3, left open here. It is a genuine production-readiness/robustness concern, not speculative or intended design. Severity is low — it surfaces a developer/integration mistake as a less-actionable exception; it is not a security or data-integrity bug, and the failure is still a hard failure (no silent bypass).

input.reasoning: Verified against current code. gates.py:87 calls self.precondition(context) and gates.py:88 immediately reads decision.approved with no type guard. precondition is a study-supplied Callable[[Mapping], GateDecision] on the adapter trust boundary (gates.py:65). A precondition that returns a non-GateDecision (e.g. lambda ctx: True) raises AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved' at line 88 rather than the module's documented GateError. The module docstring (gates.py:5-6) and GateError docstring (gates.py:37-38) guarantee GateError as the failure type for an illegal/non-approved transition. PoC is directly reproducible. The contract-asymmetry claim is also verified: pass 3 (#F-021) added the analogous isinstance guard for confirmer return values at pipeline.py:563 (and adjudication.py:255) precisely to convert this opaque-AttributeError class into a typed error, but the equivalent guard was not added for gate preconditions. This is the same defect class the team already chose to fix, left open here. Genuine robustness/production-readiness concern, not speculative and not intended design. Severity is low: it degrades a developer/integration error into a less-actionable exception type; it does not enable a silent gate bypass (the call still fails hard) and is not a security/data-integrity issue. Suggested fix (insert isinstance(decision, GateDecision) check raising GateError before line 88) is correct.

content.text: Confirmed G-019 as a true, low-severity finding. See gates.py:87-89 (missing type guard) vs. the pass-3 precedent at pipeline.py:563.
